passages.explanation: Jesus speaks to the multitudes and his disciples: the scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses' seat. So observe and do whatever they tell you, but don't follow their works — they say and don't do. They bind heavy burdens that are hard to bear and lay them on men's shoulders, but they themselves won't lift a finger. They do all their works to be seen — they make their phylacteries broad and enlarge the fringes of their garments. They love the best places at feasts, the chief seats in synagogues, greetings in the marketplaces, and being called Rabbi. But don't be called Rabbi — one is your teacher, and you're all brothers. Call no man on earth your father — one is your Father, in heaven. Don't be called masters — one is your master, the Christ. The greatest among you will be your servant. Whoever exalts himself will be humbled; whoever humbles himself will be exalted. Jesus tells the crowd to respect the office but not imitate the officeholders. Religious leadership that craves titles and visibility has already missed the point.

Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You shut up the Kingdom of Heaven against men — you don't enter, and you don't allow those entering to go in. Woe to you — you travel over sea and land to make one convert, and when he becomes one, you make him twice as much a son of Gehenna as yourselves. Woe to you, blind guides, who say whoever swears by the temple, it's nothing — but whoever swears by the gold of the temple, he's obligated. You fools and blind men — which is greater, the gold or the temple that sanctifies the gold? You say whoever swears by the altar, it's nothing — but whoever swears by the gift on it is obligated. Which is greater, the gift or the altar that sanctifies it? Jesus systematically exposes how their elaborate oath system creates categories of truth and deception. They've turned honesty into a technicality.

Woe to you — you tithe mint, dill, and cumin, and have left undone the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faith. You ought to have done these without leaving the other undone. Blind guides, who strain out a gnat and swallow a camel! Woe to you — you clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside they're full of extortion and self-indulgence. Blind Pharisee! First clean the inside of the cup, so the outside may also become clean. Woe to you — you're like whitewashed tombs, which outwardly appear beautiful but inside are full of dead men's bones and all uncleanness. Even so you also outwardly appear righteous, but inside you're full of hypocrisy and lawlessness. The precision on minor matters makes the neglect of major ones worse, not better. Meticulous tithing of garden herbs while ignoring justice is spiritual absurdity.

Woe to you — you build the tombs of the prophets and decorate the monuments of the righteous. You say: if we'd lived in our fathers' days, we wouldn't have been partakers with them in shedding the prophets' blood. So you testify against yourselves that you are sons of those who killed the prophets. Fill up the measure of your fathers. You serpents, you offspring of vipers, how will you escape the judgement of Gehenna? I send to you prophets, wise men, and scribes. Some of them you will kill and crucify. Some you will scourge in your synagogues and persecute from city to city — so that on you may come all the righteous blood shed on the earth, from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah son of Berechiah, whom you killed between the sanctuary and the altar. All these things will come on this generation. Jesus says they honour dead prophets while plotting to kill the living one standing in front of them. Nothing has changed.

Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to her! How often I wanted to gather your children together like a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you wouldn't! Behold, your house is left to you desolate. I tell you, you won't see me from now on until you say: blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord. After the most scorching denunciation in all the gospels, Jesus breaks into lament. This isn't cold judgement — it's the grief of rejected love.
